title: Psalms 29
subtitle: The Voice of the LORD over the Waters

sections.header: Verses 1–6 — “The Voice of the LORD Is Powerful”

heb.paragraph: "The voice (qōl) of the LORD is over the waters" (v.3). Seven thunders of the LORD’s voice: over waters (v.3), powerful (v.4), majestic (v.4), breaks cedars (v.5), makes Lebanon skip (v.6). The storm psalm: God’s voice IS the thunder. Each "voice of the LORD" is a thunderclap.

panels.ctx: A hymn to God’s power in the storm. The heavenly beings are called to worship (vv.1–2). Then seven "voice of the LORD" declarations trace the storm from sea to land: over waters (v.3), powerful and majestic (v.4), shattering cedars and making mountains skip (vv.5–6).

cross.note: "Listen! Listen to the roar of his voice, to the rumbling that comes from his mouth" — the same thunder theology.
"From the throne came flashes of lightning, rumblings and peals of thunder" — the heavenly storm.

notes.note: "Ascribe to the LORD glory and strength. Worship the LORD in the splendour of his holiness." The heavenly court is summoned to worship before the storm begins.
"The voice of the LORD is over the waters... breaks the cedars... makes Lebanon skip like a calf." Seven thunders. The storm tracks from sea to mountain. God’s voice reshapes geography.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

sections.header: Verses 7–11 — “The LORD Sits Enthroned over the Flood”

heb.paragraph: "The LORD sits enthroned over the flood (mabbūl); the LORD is enthroned as King forever" (v.10). Mabbūl is used elsewhere only for Noah’s flood (Genesis 6–9). God who sent the flood also rules over it. Chaos is not uncontrolled; it is governed.

panels.ctx: More thunders: flashes of lightning (v.7), shakes the desert (v.8), twists oaks and strips forests (v.9). "In his temple all cry, ‘Glory!’" (v.9). Then the throne: God sits enthroned over the flood — the Noah-flood word. The God who unleashed chaos also governs it. "The LORD gives strength to his people; the LORD blesses his people with peace" (v.11). The storm ends in shalom.

cross.note: The mabbūl (flood) — the only other occurrence of this word.
"The seas have lifted up their voice... mightier than the breakers of the sea — the LORD on high is mighty" — the same enthronement-over-waters theology.

notes.note: "In his temple all cry, ‘Glory!’" The storm produces worship. The appropriate response to God’s power is not fear but praise.
"The LORD sits enthroned over the flood. The LORD blesses his people with peace." From storm to peace. From chaos to shalom. The psalm ends where all tempests end: in God’s sovereign calm.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

ppl.name: God
ppl.text: Then the throne: God sits enthroned over the flood — the Noah-flood word…

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVThe voice of the Lord twists the oaks and strips the forests bare. And in his temple all cry, “Glory!”ESVThe voice of the LORD makes the deer give birth and strips the forests bare, and in his temple all cry, \u201cGlory!\u201dNIVThe voice of the Lord is over the waters; the God of glory thunders, the Lord thunders over the mighty waters.ESVThe voice of the LORD is over the waters; the God of glory thunders, the LORD, over many waters.

rec.text: ""Ascribe to the LORD glory and strength. Worship the LORD in the splendour of his holiness." The heavenly court is summoned to worship before the storm begins."

rows.label: The Throne — 29:7–11
rows.text: Enthroned over THE flood. Storm → "Glory!" → peace. Chaos governed.

hebtext.note: "The voice (qōl) of the LORD is over the waters" (v.3). Seven thunders of the LORD’s voice: over waters (v.3), powerful (v.4), majestic (v.4), breaks cedars (v.5), makes Lebanon skip (v.6). The storm psalm: God’s voice IS the thunder. Each "voice of the LORD" is a thunderclap.

"The LORD sits enthroned over the flood (mabbūl); the LORD is enthroned as King forever" (v.10). Mabbūl is used elsewhere only for Noah’s flood (Genesis 6–9). God who sent the flood also rules over it. Chaos is not uncontrolled; it is governed.

themes.note: Sovereignty peaks at 10: seven thunders + enthroned over the flood. Worship at 9: "all cry Glory!" Holiness: the splendour of holiness.
